query:
INSERT INTO `program_lessons` (programId, day, title, concept, exercisePrompt, guidanceTemplate, `order`) VALUES
(30002, 8, 'Is It True?', 'Byron Katie built an entire philosophy on four questions. The first one is the most powerful: "Is it true?" Not "is it possible" or "could it be true sometimes" — is it absolutely, undeniably, 100% true? Most of the time, the honest answer is no.', 'Take the core lie you identified on Day 3. Write it at the top of the page. Then answer: What is the evidence FOR this belief? What is the evidence AGAINST it? If a jury heard both sides, what would they decide?', 'Mirror Conversation Starter: "The belief I want to challenge today is... and when I actually look at the evidence, I find..." | Micro-Habit: When your inner critic makes a statement today, ask it one question: "Can you prove that?" Don''t argue. Just ask. Let the silence answer.', 8),
(30002, 9, 'Whose Voice Is It Really?', 'Much of what we call our "inner voice" is actually an internalized echo of someone else — a parent, a teacher, an ex, a culture. Today we do an attribution audit. You deserve to know which thoughts are actually yours.', 'Think of three critical things your inner voice says regularly. For each one, ask: "Who first said this to me, or made me feel this?" Write the name or description. Then ask: "Do I actually agree with this person''s judgment of me?"', 'Mirror Conversation Starter: "When I trace my harshest self-criticism back to its source, I find that it sounds a lot like... and what I want to say back to that voice is..." | Micro-Habit: Pick one recurring self-critical thought and literally say out loud: "That''s [name]''s voice, not mine."', 9),
(30002, 10, 'The Worst Case Isn''t What You Think', 'The inner critic is a catastrophist. It takes a moment of failure and projects it forward into a permanent identity. Today we follow the catastrophe to its actual end — and discover it''s survivable.', 'What is the worst-case scenario your inner voice is most afraid of? Write it out fully — don''t soften it. Then ask: if that actually happened, what would you do? How would you survive it? What would you learn? Who would you become on the other side?', 'Mirror Conversation Starter: "The thing my inner voice is most afraid of is... and if I''m honest, I think I could survive it because..." | Micro-Habit: Write down one thing you''ve already survived that you once thought would break you. Carry that evidence with you today.', 10),
(30002, 11, 'The Identity Underneath', 'The inner critic attacks identity — not just behavior. It doesn''t say "you did something bad." It says "you ARE bad." Today we separate what you do from who you are. This is one of the most important distinctions a human being can make.', 'List five things your inner voice says about who you ARE (not what you did). For each one, ask: "Is this a permanent trait, or is this a pattern I developed in response to specific circumstances?" There is a massive difference between the two.', 'Mirror Conversation Starter: "My inner voice often tells me that I AM... but when I look honestly at my life, what I see is actually..." | Micro-Habit: Replace one "I am" statement with "I have been acting as if." Feel the difference.', 11),
(30002, 12, 'The Friend Test', 'You would never speak to someone you love the way your inner critic speaks to you. That gap — between how you treat others and how you treat yourself — is where self-compassion lives. Today we close it.', 'Imagine your closest friend came to you with the exact situation you''re most self-critical about right now. Write out what you would say to them — every word of comfort, perspective, and encouragement. Then read it back as if it was written to you.', 'Mirror Conversation Starter: "If my best friend was going through exactly what I''m going through right now, I would tell them..." | Micro-Habit: Text one person today and tell them something you genuinely appreciate about them. Then say the same thing to yourself in the mirror. Out loud.', 12),
(30002, 13, 'Rewriting the Headline', 'Your inner critic writes tabloid headlines about your life — sensational, one-sided, stripped of context. Today we become editors. We don''t delete the story. We rewrite the headline to reflect the full truth.', 'Write three "tabloid headlines" your inner voice would write about you right now. Then rewrite each one as a fair, accurate, full-context headline — the kind a wise, compassionate journalist would write. Notice how different the story feels.', 'Mirror Conversation Starter: "The headline my inner voice would write about my life right now is... but the more accurate headline is..." | Micro-Habit: Every time you catch a catastrophic thought today, mentally say "REWRITE." Just flag it. The awareness is the work.', 13),
(30002, 14, 'Week 2 Integration', 'You''ve spent a week cross-examining the voice. You''ve asked it for evidence, traced its origins, followed its worst-case scenarios to their survivable ends. The voice is still there — but it has less authority now. You''ve seen behind the curtain. Today we mark that shift.', 'Write about the single most powerful realization from this week. What did you discover about your inner critic that you couldn''t see before? How has your relationship with that voice shifted — even slightly?', 'Mirror Conversation Starter: "The biggest shift I''ve noticed in how I relate to my inner voice this week is..." | Micro-Habit: Go back to the letter you wrote on Day 7. Read it again. Then write one sentence at the bottom: "And now I also know..."', 14);
command:
